# Transcrição: 3. The Lunch Opportunity

- **Fonte:** 3. The Lunch Opportunity
- **Duração:** 05:45
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hey, this is called the lunch opportunity. I promise you I talk about this, how to take that lunch opportunity, that one hour, hour and a half, 45 minutes and turn it into money. Now, I learned this valuable lesson back when I was a salesperson wasting lunches with my coworker, Gene. Today, I won't go to lunch with a fellow salesperson. Hell, I won't even go with a manager. I'm not even interested in going with my boss. I need to spend time with customers, people that can buy things. I don't need to win my boss over anymore. You got the job already. How do you keep the job? Revenue. You understand? Revenue. Produce revenue. Assist in producing revenue. And that means I need clients. Right? Smoothing your boss is not going to get you job security. Real security comes from production. Okay? Now, my rule is this. If you work with me and you can't buy from me and you're not going to buy from me, okay, I don't spend lunch with you. Man, Grant, you're so harsh. No, look, I know what I want. I want to take care of my family. I want to take care of my kids. I want to take care of my future. And I want to one day have a great life. That means I need to take advantage of time. You need to work your sales career the way a politician works his campaign. He doesn't keep talking to people that already are going to vote for him, right? He goes out and talks to people that haven't yet voted for him. Today, I invest my lunchtime, my breakfast time, and my dinner time with buyers, prospects, even long shots. These lunch dates would include anyone who might someday buy from me or buy from my company or would allow me to reach up into society, not just sideways and down. You understand what I'm talking about? I want to reach up, not down. I want to use lunch, dinners, breakfasts, every opportunity. It doesn't mean my wife can't come with me. She can. We can do lunch together with a client. Even when I'm not taking a customer to a lunch, I'll frequent places where I've got a shot at being seen. It's called nesting. Repetitive visits to the same place over and over and over again until you know everyone there. That's a way to use your lunch. That way I know the owner of the company. I know the manager of the company. I know the waiters and waitresses in the company. I know the chef. I know everybody there. It's called nesting and you want to repeat that. Those are going to become contacts. People that go out for lunch are typically qualified buyers. There are people who are working, bankers, insurance people, salespeople, other salespeople, entrepreneurs, CEOs, movers, shakers, producers, directors. These are the buyers of your products. They go out. You want to go out and be with them. There's no money in your office, folks. Get out of the office. But don't go with other employees. Go out to be seen by others. Get to know these people. Find a restaurant where people are qualified to go out. Find a restaurant where people come together that are qualified financially.

## [03:03 - 05:45]

Don't go to the cheapest place in town. Reach up. I mean, come on. Who are you going to meet at Kentucky Fried Chicken? They go in, they're out. It's not fast food restaurants you want to go to if you're going to be seen. You want to, again, you want to go back and visit that one place over and over and over, nest, until you know everyone there. Again, get to know the owner. Get to know the people there. Then you'll get to know the patrons. Go to the places where potential customers congregate at lunch and be seen by them. Now, personally, I like to go to the pricier places because I know they attract quality customers. The great stock of the Nazis, the great shipping magnet. One of the wealthiest people, he created from a self-made guy, created tremendous amounts of wealth. He always made a point, and has said, always made a point to go to the most expensive restaurants throughout his travels, even when he was a young man before he was extremely wealthy. Not because he could afford it, but because he knew, hey, the people in those restaurants have money. He wanted to be around opportunity. He wanted to be around money, and he wanted to be around success. I once made a sale to an insurance agent. Three days after I sold the product I sold to him, I called him up and said, hey, I'd like to do lunch with you, as a sign of my appreciation. I drove 30 minutes, about 25 miles. I met him in his office. I loaded up his wife and his daughter and went off to his favorite place for lunch. At the time I was thinking, I was small, thinking small, I'm worried about the money, I'm worried about the bill, I'm thinking, oh gosh, how much is it going to be? Let me tell you what happened. I sit down with this guy, his wife and his daughter. I literally sit down, I'm there maybe 30 seconds, no more than a minute. Hey, my customer says, Vic, Vic, this is the kid I was telling you about. Vic reaches in his pocket, pulls out a card and says, shh, call me. In fact, you know what? I just want one just like him. I want to tell you exactly what I want, just like what Enos bought, have it delivered to my office. I'm like, you got to be kidding, man. This was easy. How did I get that sale? And if I had not picked up my client, his wife and his daughter and gone to a place where he congregates, we wouldn't have met Vic. Vic wouldn't have given me the card and I wouldn't have gotten lucky. So you guys out there, ladies and gentlemen, you people out there, they're like, I'm so unlucky, that never happens to me. It doesn't happen to you because you're eating in your office. Get out into the workplace. Get out where money is exchanged every day, where people are meeting people. Okay? Turn this lunch opportunity into money for you.
